body_txt: When a story spans decades, it is often not possible for the one actor to play the same character throughout. Heavy make-up might just let the 30 year old play 90 , but never under 18 . Instead, multiple actors are used for the same role. A time-shifted actor will usually be credited as playing 'Young Mr. X' or 'Old Ms. Y'. Differentiated from the other actor replacement tropes by the passage of long stretches of story-internal time. If those long stretches of time have to pass during a short stretch of footage, an Age Cut will allow the actors to be swapped quickly. If the passage of time happens in both real life and fiction, it overlaps with Character Aged with the Actor . This is especially popular in prequels and sequels , or in stories with a Framing Device in which 'Old Ms. Y' recalls events of her younger days. Sometimes the necessary resemblance between Mr. X and Young Mr. X will be achieved by use of a Real-Life Relative , which equals Age Them Down, Cast Their Kid . If only a photograph is required, an old picture of the actor/actress will suffice. Compare and Contrast Role Reprise . See also Identical Grandson . Less common than it used to be due to Digital De-Aging becoming more frequently used. It's still used in some instances , such as when adult characters are portrayed as children or vice-versa. Voiceover work also contains plenty of instances of this, and in that case is a subtrope of Two Voices, One Character .
